I mean, we've had moments, plenty of them, but none with a memorable soundtrack. It's not like we didn't listen to music, but a particular song hadn't stepped forward to claim us. These memories have been cherished throughout many late-night sessions or while driving long distances in hopes that the details won't be forgotten. "All of life I've wanted to become a Patriot, Bang!bang,bang baby bang!" After spending about two weeks on campus during our fall 1993 semester at Valley Forge Christian College (VFC), Rachel and Jamee introduced me into an unspoken club. The first night we met up in the cafeteria after lunch was when I learned what they were all thinking so excitedly of: a game called "Bang!". The rules are simple, but it took me several hours to get everyone's hang on them and how much fun this would be for our late-night sessions. The goal is pretty clear - survive as long you can by shooting at the other players before they have their chance (which was limited) of getting a shot back against your own. The game, however simple it might seem now in writing down its rules above; we found ourselves laughing and yelling with excitement until our throats hurt. It was something that everyone looked forward to during those days when school started off slow or if they had been having some rough days on campus (which is not uncommon). As the semester progressed, more students joined into this "Bang!"

The raven motif on Sir Ugyen Wangchuck’s crown is deeply rooted in Bhutanese mythology. According to legend, the protective deity Mahakala often appears as a raven during times of crisis or significant change for the kingdom and its people. By incorporating this powerful symbol into his regalia, King Ugyen was not only claiming divine right but also demonstrating that he had the favor and protection of Bhutan’s most revered guardian spirit. This unique piece became an enduring emblem of royal authority in Bhutan; every subsequent king has worn a version of it during formal ceremonies. The Raven Crown is more than just ceremonial—it serves as a tangible link between the monarchy, its people, and their shared spiritual heritage. It symbolizes both temporal power and divine sanction, reinforcing the sacred nature of leadership within this Himalayan kingdom. The story behind Bhutan's Raven Crown highlights how deeply interwoven mythology can be with political symbolism in cultures that maintain strong ties to traditional beliefs and practices. In a way, it serves as an invitation for those who wear or behold it: remember your duties towards both the earthly realm you govern and the spiritual forces upon which its stability ultimately depends. Sixty-eight patients who underwent Harrington hip arthroplasties for metastatic cancer from August 2005 to March 2015 were identified. All patients had a constrained acetabular liner implanted during their index procedure. Electronic medical records and radiographs were reviewed. A literature review of studies with similar technique was used as a historical control. Forty-seven patients (69.2%) died 22.7 ± 22.7 months after surgery, at age 62.4 ± 17.2 years.
